quick_facts.label: What it is
quick_facts.value: LL.M. (Master of Laws) is a postgraduate law programme pursued after an LL.B. or equivalent law qualification; the exact programme structure and specialisations are set by the institution.

quick_facts.label: CLAT-PG eligibility (2026 cycle)
quick_facts.value: For CLAT-PG 2026, candidates needed an LL.B. or equivalent with at least 50% marks (45% for SC/ST/PwD candidates); there was no upper age limit. Candidates taking the qualifying examination in March/April 2026 could apply provisionally, subject to producing proof of passing at admission.

quick_facts.label: NLU admission route
quick_facts.value: CLAT-PG is the common admission test for LL.M. programmes at participating NLUs in the 2026–27 cycle. Participation, seat matrices, reservation, specialisations and fee rules are university-specific and may change by admission cycle.

quick_facts.label: Other entrance route
quick_facts.value: CUET-PG 2026 included the Law/LLM test paper COQP14, and universities using CUET-PG apply their own programme eligibility and admissions rules.

quick_facts.label: Duration
quick_facts.value: Duration is institution-specific: current official examples include one-year LL.M. programmes, while some universities list a four-semester programme.

sections.heading: Eligibility
paragraphs: An LL.M. ordinarily requires an undergraduate law degree, rather than merely any bachelor’s degree. For the CLAT-PG 2026 route used by participating NLUs, the Consortium required an LL.B. degree or equivalent with 50% marks, reducing to 45% for SC/ST/PwD candidates; no upper age limit applied. Final-year law students could apply, but had to furnish evidence of passing when required for admission.

Institutions can impose or describe their own eligibility conditions. For example, NLSIU’s 2026–27 LL.M. page specifies an LL.B. or equivalent and 50% for General/OBC/PwD candidates and 45% for SC/ST candidates, while Nirma’s 2026–27 programme required a three-year LL.B. after graduation or a five-year integrated law degree recognised by the Bar Council of India, with at least 50% aggregate marks.
bullets: Check the qualifying-degree requirement, category relaxation, recognition requirements, document deadline and any special-category rules in the admission notice of each target university.
Do not assume that meeting the CLAT-PG minimum alone guarantees admission: the relevant university’s seat matrix, reservation and counselling rules also apply.

sections.heading: Duration and format
paragraphs: There is no single duration that can safely be assumed for every Indian LL.M. Current official university examples show both formats: NLSIU describes a one-year LL.M., and Dr. Rammanohar Lohia Avadh University lists an LL.M. with four semesters. Applicants should therefore confirm duration, academic calendar, attendance rules and whether the programme is full-time before applying.
bullets: Four-semester example: Dr. Rammanohar Lohia Avadh University LL.M. (SFS).

sections.heading: Curriculum and specialisation
paragraphs: LL.M. study is typically advanced and research-oriented, but course design is university-specific. NLSIU’s published curriculum illustrates a structure combining research methodology, comparative public law, a concentration core, concentration and general electives, and a dissertation; it states that 48 credits are required, including four dissertation credits. Its listed concentrations include Criminal Law, Commercial Laws, and Law and Technology.

Specialisation offerings can differ sharply across institutions. For example, KIIT School of Law lists Corporate and Commercial Laws, Technology and Intellectual Property Laws, Constitutional and Administrative Laws, Criminal Law and Criminal Justice Administration, Family Laws, and Public and Private International Laws for its 2026–27 one-year LL.M.
bullets: Read the current curriculum and credit rules rather than choosing only by the degree title.
Where a dissertation is compulsory, align the proposed area with intended research, teaching or policy interests.

sections.heading: Admission routes and preparation
paragraphs: For the 2026–27 NLU admissions cycle, CLAT 2026 was the route for LL.M. programmes at participating universities. The Consortium states that admissions information such as intake, reservation, courses and fees must be checked in the relevant NLU brochure and on the university’s own website; an allotment is provisional and does not itself create a right to admission.

CLAT-PG 2026 had a 120-minute paper with 120 objective questions, with negative marking of 0.25 for each incorrect answer. The syllabus was based on compulsory undergraduate-law subjects, including Constitutional Law, Jurisprudence, Administrative Law, Contract, Torts, Family Law, Criminal Law, Property, Company Law, Public International Law, Tax, Environmental Law, and Labour and Industrial Law.

CLAT-PG is not the only LL.M. route. CUET-PG 2026 included COQP14 for Law/LLM, and the University of Delhi stated that admission to its two-year postgraduate programmes for academic year 2026–27 would be based on CUET-PG 2026. Candidates must still apply through the receiving university’s own admission process where required.
bullets: For participating NLUs: follow the Consortium’s current CLAT-PG schedule, then complete counselling and university-specific verification steps.
For CUET-PG and other university routes: verify the required test-paper code, the university’s LL.M. eligibility, and its separate registration/seat-allocation procedure.
Treat dates, participating institutions, counselling steps and test patterns as cycle-specific.

sections.heading: Costs and financial planning
paragraphs: Fees vary substantially by institution, category, residence arrangement and academic year; they should not be treated as a national LL.M. price. As dated 2026–27 examples, NLSIU published an annual total cost to student of ₹5,06,400 for its PG programmes, comprising ₹3,18,200 tuition and facilities fees plus ₹1,88,200 off-campus housing fees, with separate one-time admission and refundable security charges. Nirma published a 2026–27 total amount payable of ₹2,11,250 for its LL.M. admission-year schedule, excluding separately stated hostel, mess and laundry charges.

These examples are context only, not fee benchmarks. Before accepting an offer, separate tuition, mandatory university charges, refundable deposits, accommodation, food, examination charges, travel, books and any scholarship or category-based concession.
bullets: NLSIU figures are stated per annum and include an off-campus housing component.
Nirma’s published total includes tuition and listed institutional charges, while hostel, mess and laundry are separately payable.
Always use the applicable admission-year fee notice and refund policy of the university.

sections.heading: What an LL.M. can lead to
paragraphs: An LL.M. can support a research and academic pathway. Under the UGC Ph.D. Regulations, 2022, master’s-degree holders with at least 55% marks or equivalent are eligible to seek Ph.D. admission, subject to the regulations and the institution’s process; the regulations allow a 5% relaxation for specified categories. UGC norms for Assistant Professor appointments in Law also identify a relevant master’s degree with 55% marks together with NET/SLET/SET (unless an applicable Ph.D. exemption applies).

UGC-NET is therefore a relevant next examination for an LL.M. graduate aiming at teaching or research, but candidates must check the current NTA bulletin for the applicable session and subject requirements. An LL.M. is not required for AIBE: the official AIBE site states that eligibility is based on a BCI-recognised three- or five-year LL.B. (including specified final-year/final-semester candidates), so an LL.M. neither substitutes for nor is necessary to satisfy the underlying LL.B. requirement.

For practice-oriented, in-house, public-sector, judicial-service, JAG or research roles, an LL.M. may add subject-matter depth, but recruitment eligibility is set separately by the appointing authority. Verify each notification for required LL.B., bar enrolment/AIBE status, experience, age, examination and category conditions.
bullets: Academic/research route: LL.M. → UGC-NET and/or Ph.D. admission, subject to prevailing eligibility and institutional rules.
Practice route: LL.B. recognition and AIBE conditions remain independently relevant; do not assume an LL.M. changes AIBE eligibility.
Employment and competitive-exam routes: use the issuing employer’s or recruiting authority’s current notification as the controlling source.
